commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.review_summary:
The branch adds fast-path mapped Telegram callback toasts and visible acknowledgements for final reply button payloads, plus callback-focused Telegram tests.

PR surface: Source +74, Tests +169. Total +243 across 4 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for the blocking PR defect by source inspection: PR head sends a visible Telegram message in `bot-core.ts` before the callback handler reaches scope and sender authorization in `bot-handlers.runtime.ts`. I did not run tests because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: A focused repair can move visible acknowledgements behind the existing Telegram callback authorization path and add the missing unauthorized-callback regression coverage.

target.title: telegram: fast-ack final button callbacks
target.body:
Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (Yes/No)

No

What is the highest-risk area?

Telegram callback handling, specifically the new fast-lane acknowledgment path that runs before the normal sequentialized callback processing.

How is that risk mitigated?

The change is narrowly scoped to known final-button callback payloads and keeps the normal callback handler in place.
Mitigations:
- callback payloads are explicitly matched, including legacy values
- the fast lane marks context so the normal handler does not send duplicate visible acknowledgments
- the normal handler still provides visible fallback behavior if answerCallbackQuery is stale or fails
- targeted callback-focused Telegram tests passed after updating adjacent callback expectations
- local lint on the touched Telegram files passed after the cleanup for CI style rules

<details>
<summary>Risk guidance</summary>

The main behavioral risk is callback acknowledgment ordering: this change intentionally moves acknowledgment earlier for supported final-button presses so users get immediate feedback.

Reviewers should verify:
- only the intended final-button payloads take the fast lane
- callback toasts and visible thread messages use the mapped text
- duplicate visible acknowledgments are suppressed when the fast lane already sent one
- stale callback cases still surface the visible fallback message
- unrelated callback flows continue to receive the generic Received callback toast contract

</details>

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review the Telegram callback flow changes in `extensions/telegram/src/bot-core.ts` and `extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts`, with attention to ordering and duplicate-ack suppression.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Author follow-up from the first CI pass:
- fix repo lint/style violations in the touched Telegram files
- add this real-behavior-proof section

Local verification after that cleanup:
- callback-focused Telegram tests passed (`166` tests)
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/telegram/src/bot-core.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts` passed

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Addressed the concern that the acknowledgment path should be implemented in upstream source rather than as ad-hoc local compiled-file patches.
Also added test coverage for:
- pre-sequentialization final-button acknowledgment
- no duplicate visible acknowledgment after fast-lane ack
- visible fallback when callback toast delivery is stale/fails

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:
  Telegram final reply buttons should acknowledge immediately on tap instead of waiting behind normal per-chat sequentialized processing.

- Real environment tested:
  A live OpenClaw Telegram group chat running the equivalent local Telegram callback patch that this PR ports into upstream source.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Open a Telegram thread with a final assistant reply that includes the `Proceed`, `Status`, or `Provide recommendation` buttons.
  2. Tap one of those buttons in the live chat.
  3. Observe whether the callback toast and visible thread acknowledgment appear immediately, before the longer follow-up response.

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  After-fix screenshot evidence from the live Telegram chat shows the button-driven follow-up path in use and the expected final-button UX flow. The live thread where this behavior was validated is the same Telegram setup that motivated the upstream source port for this PR.
  Copied live output from the validated source-port branch:
  - callback-focused validation: `166 passed (166)`
  - lint on touched Telegram files: `pnpm exec oxlint ...` passed

- Observed result after fix:
  Tapping a supported final button produces the mapped immediate acknowledgment text (`Proceeding...`, `Checking status...`, or `Preparing recommendation...`) without waiting for the full downstream response, and the normal handler does not emit a duplicate visible acknowledgment when the fast lane already sent it.

- What was not tested:
  I did not run a fresh end-to-end live Telegram tap against this exact upstream checkout from the PR branch on this host. I also did not rerun the full Telegram extension lane after rebasing because unrelated `bot-message-context*` failures on current `upstream/main` are outside this callback change.

- Proof limitations or environment constraints:
  The real-user behavior was validated on the live local OpenClaw Telegram setup that originally carried the local patch, then ported into this upstream source branch. The PR branch itself was verified locally with focused callback tests and lint, but I do not have a separate disposable live Telegram deployment wired directly to this exact PR branch from this CI host.

- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
  Before the fast-lane path, callback acknowledgment for these final buttons waited behind the normal sequentialized callback flow, which made button taps feel delayed.
